content.text: Term Definition EASE Evaluation of Achievement Standardized Exam DTP Development of Teaching Proficiency (also called Educamp) EduNav Eduversal's School Information System EduOS Eduversal Olimpiade Sains (Science Olympiad preparation) Dapodik Data Pokok Pendidikan (National Education Database) NPSN Nomor Pokok Sekolah Nasional (National School ID Number) BAN-PDM National School Accreditation Board ANBK Asesmen Nasional Berbasis Komputer (Computer-Based National Assessment) PPKn Pendidikan Pancasila dan Kewarganegaraan (Civics Education) NISN Nomor Induk Siswa Nasional (National Student ID) NUPTK Nomor Unik Pendidik dan Tenaga Kependidikan (Teacher ID) NIK Nomor Induk Kependudukan (National ID Card Number) KITAS Kartu Izin Tinggal Terbatas (Limited Stay Permit) KITAP Kartu Izin Tinggal Tetap (Permanent Stay Permit) IMTA Izin Mempekerjakan Tenaga Kerja Asing (Work Permit) PPh 21 Personal Income Tax Withholding PPh 23 Withholding Tax on Services BPJS Badan Penyelenggara Jaminan Sosial (Social Security) SLO Schoolwide Learner Outcome GM General Manager SLT Senior Leadership Team DSL Designated Safeguarding Lead SIP School Improvement Plan PAS Penilaian Akhir Semester (End of Semester Assessment)

To ensure full adherence to Indonesian national regulations governing private education institutions, maintain all required licenses and permits, integrate mandatory national curriculum components, and fulfill reporting obligations to the Ministry of Education, Culture, Research, and Technology (Kemendikdasmen).

Requirement: Each Eduversal Partner School operates under a legally registered Yayasan (foundation) as the school owner and license holder, in accordance with Law No. 28 of 2004 concerning Foundations (as amended by Law No. 16 of 2001). Key Obligations:

items: Akta Pendirian (Deed of Establishment): Notarized foundation charter registered with the Ministry of Law and Human Rights
NPWP Yayasan: Tax identification number for the foundation
Annual Reporting: Submit annual activity and financial reports to the relevant authorities
Asset Management: All school buildings, land, and facilities owned or controlled by the Yayasan
Non-Profit Status: Operate exclusively for educational and social purposes; any surplus reinvested in educational quality
content.title: Yayasan (Foundation) Legal Status

items: Notarized Akta Pendirian and any amendments
Registration certificate from the Ministry of Law and Human Rights
NPWP certificate
Annual financial statements audited by a registered accountant
Board meeting minutes documenting oversight

content.text: Requirement: Each school level (PAUD, SD, SMP, SMA) must obtain and maintain an operational license from the local Education Office (Dinas Pendidikan Kabupaten/Kota or Provinsi). Process:

steps: Application Submission: Completed application with supporting documents to local Dinas Pendidikan
Document Requirements:
content.title: School Operational Licenses (Izin Operasional)

items: Yayasan legal documents (Akta, registration)
Land ownership/lease documentation with minimum area compliance
Building safety certificate (IMB \- Izin Mendirikan Bangunan)
Fire safety clearance
Environmental health clearance (Puskesmas)
Proposed curriculum and staffing plan
Principal and teacher qualification documents

steps: Site Inspection: Dinas team verifies facilities, safety, and readiness
Issuance: Operational license valid for 5 years (renewable)

items: Submit renewal application 6 months before expiration
Updated documentation and compliance evidence
Site re-inspection as needed
Current operational license (Izin Operasional) for each school level
Renewal application tracking log
Inspection reports and corrective action records
Correspondence with Dinas Pendidikan

content.text: Requirement: Every school must obtain a unique NPSN from the Ministry of Education as the official school identifier for all national reporting systems. Purpose:

items: National school registry and identification
Access to Dapodik (national data system)
Eligibility for government programs and subsidies (if applicable)
UN/ANBK examination registration
content.title: NPSN (Nomor Pokok Sekolah Nasional)

items: Submit online via Dapodik system
Provide school profile, operational license, and facility data
NPSN issued upon verification
Keep NPSN data current in Dapodik
Report any changes (address, principal, accreditation status)
NPSN certificate and documentation
Dapodik registration confirmation

content.text: Purpose: Dapodik is the Ministry's primary database for schools, students, teachers, and facilities. It serves as the foundation for national education planning, funding allocation, and policy decisions. Responsibilities: School-Level (Operator Dapodik):

items: Designate a trained Dapodik Operator (staff member responsible for data entry)
Update data each semester (minimum):
Student enrollment and attendance
Teacher and staff employment data
Curriculum delivery and assessment results
Facility and infrastructure inventory
Financial data (for schools receiving government funds)
Synchronize data online with the national server
Respond to validation errors and queries
content.title: Dapodik (Data Pokok Pendidikan)

items: Ensure Dapodik Operator receives training and support
Review data quality and accuracy before synchronization
Approve data submissions
Maintain documentation of data sources and changes
Semester 1 (odd): Data cut-off October 31, sync by November 15
Semester 2 (even): Data cut-off April 30, sync by May 15
Ad hoc updates: As required for critical changes (e.g., new student registrations, teacher appointments)

content.text: Note: Sync deadlines align with national reporting requirements and precede end-of-semester assessments (PAS). Common Data Elements:

items: Students: Name, NISN (national student ID), birthdate, address, parent contact, enrollment date, class placement
Teachers: Name, NIK (ID card), NUPTK (teacher ID if applicable), qualifications, subjects taught, employment status
Curriculum: Subjects offered, teaching hours, assessment methods
Facilities: Classrooms, labs, libraries, sports facilities, and condition
Dapodik Operator appointment letter and training certificates
Data entry logs and synchronization receipts
Validation reports and error correction documentation
Annual Dapodik accuracy audit by GM or designated reviewer

content.text: Purpose: SIMPKB tracks teacher professional development activities and is linked to teacher certification and career progression. Requirements:

items: All certified teachers (with NRG \- Nomor Registrasi Guru) must maintain SIMPKB accounts
Record all CPD activities: workshops, training, conferences, lesson study, action research
Upload supporting evidence (certificates, attendance records)
Minimum CPD hours per year as mandated by regulations
content.title: SIMPKB (Sistem Informasi Manajemen Pengembangan Keprofesian Berkelanjutan)

items: Facilitate teacher access to SIMPKB
Verify and endorse teacher CPD claims
Organize school-based professional development that qualifies for SIMPKB credit
Maintain school records of PD activities as backup documentation
SIMPKB activity logs for all certified teachers
School PD calendar and attendance records
Certificates of completion for workshops/training
Annual CPD summary report

content.text: Purpose: SIAGA is an integrated information system managed by the Ministry of Religious Affairs (Kementerian Agama) to administer and verify data of religion teachers serving in public and private schools. It ensures valid and up-to-date records for teacher appointment verification, professional allowance eligibility, and career development. Requirements:

items: All religion teachers, both ASN and non-ASN, must have an active SIAGA account.
Teachers are required to regularly update their personal and professional data, including:
Identity and employment data (NIK, NUPTK, education background, certification, and employment status)
School assignment and teaching load (subject, number of teaching hours, grade level)
Certification data and current verification status
Uploaded supporting documents (SK Pengangkatan, SK Pembagian Tugas, Ijazah, Sertifikat Pendidik, etc.)
Record of training, workshops, and professional development activities
content.title: SIAGA (Sistem Informasi dan Administrasi Guru Agama)

items: Register and activate a SIAGA account through the designated Pengawas PAI at the Ministry of Religious Affairs office (Kemenag Kabupaten/Kota).
Maintain accuracy and completeness of personal and professional data in the SIAGA system.
Upload all required supporting documents for verification.
Check notifications regarding data validation, certification, and allowance eligibility.
Issue an official introduction letter (surat pengantar) stating that the teacher is assigned as a PAI teacher at the school (If needed).
Attach relevant supporting documents such as SK Pengangkatan/SK Penetapan, and SK Pembagian Tugas.
Provide school verification when requested by the Kemenag office.
Support data consistency between the school's Dapodik record and the teacher’s SIAGA account.
Account Activation: Upon teacher’s initial assignment as religion teacher (new or transfer case).
Data Update: Each semester or whenever there are changes in assignment, teaching load, or personal data.
Professional Allowance Verification: According to Kemenag’s scheduled verification cycle.
SK Pengangkatan / SK Penetapan, and SK Pembagian Tugas.
SIAGA account activation confirmation from Pengawas / Operator Kemenag.
Printed SIAGA teacher profile (updated).
Semesterly SIAGA verification report or validation record.

content.text: Requirement: While Eduversal Partner Schools follow the Cambridge curriculum framework, they must integrate mandatory Indonesian national curriculum subjects and embrace the Profil Pelajar Pancasila competencies as outlined in Kurikulum National (Merdeka). Mandatory National Subjects: All students must receive instruction in:

steps: Pendidikan Pancasila dan Kewarganegaraan (PPKn) \- Civics and Pancasila Education
content.title: Kurikulum Nasional with Profil Pelajar Pancasila

items: Minimum 2 hours/week across all levels
Covers Indonesian national values, citizenship, constitution, and rights/responsibilities

steps: Bahasa Indonesia

items: Minimum 4-6 hours/week (varies by level)
National language proficiency, literature, and communication

steps: Pendidikan Agama \- Religious Education

items: Minimum 2-3 hours/week
Students grouped by religion (Islam, Protestant, Catholic, Hindu, Buddhist, Confucian)
Qualified religious education teachers for each faith

steps: Muatan Lokal (Mulok) \- Local Content

items: Subjects reflecting local culture, language, or context
Determined in consultation with local Dinas Pendidikan
Examples: Javanese language, Sundanese arts, local history, environmental studies
National subjects are taught in dedicated time blocks
Use Indonesian as the language of instruction
Follow national curriculum standards and learning objectives
Assess using national frameworks (not Cambridge)
Student reports include separate sections for:
Cambridge curriculum subjects (English language)
National curriculum subjects (Indonesian language)
Profil Pelajar Pancasila competencies tracked and reported
Typical weekly allocation:
Cambridge subjects: \~70% of instructional time
National subjects: \~25% of instructional time
Co-curricular/enrichment: \~5%

content.text: What is EduSTEAM? EduSTEAM is Eduversal's signature pedagogical approach integrating Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, and Mathematics through project-based learning. Key Features:

steps: Design Thinking methodology (Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test)
Maker education with hands-on prototyping
Real-world problem solving with authentic community connections
Integration with Cambridge inquiry skills and Indonesian character education

content.text: EduSTEAM serves as the delivery model for projects, ensuring students develop both Profil Pelajar Pancasila competencies and 21st-century STEAM skills simultaneously.

Purpose: Profil Pelajar Pancasila defines the competencies Indonesian students should develop, aligned with Pancasila values. While the Cambridge curriculum drives academic content, it shapes character education and student development programs. EduSTEAM Integration: Eduversal implements EduSTEAM (Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts, Mathematics) as its signature interdisciplinary learning approach across all partner schools. EduSTEAM provides the pedagogical framework and methodology for delivering Profil Pelajar Pancasila projects, ensuring students develop:

steps: Interdisciplinary thinking through integrated STEAM challenges
Real-world problem-solving using design thinking methodologies
Innovation and creativity through hands-on prototyping and experimentation
Critical reasoning via inquiry-based investigation
Collaboration through team-based project work
content.title: Profil Pelajar Pancasila

content.text: The Profil Pelajar Pancasila program at Eduversal schools is delivered through the EduSTEAM approach, combining:

steps: Indonesian national character competencies (Profil Pelajar Pancasila dimensions)
International STEAM pedagogies (project-based learning, design thinking, maker education)
Cambridge inquiry skills (research, analysis, evidence-based reasoning)

content.text: This alignment ensures students develop both national character values and 21st-century competencies through authentic, engaging learning experiences. The Six Dimensions of Profil Pelajar Pancasila:

steps: Beriman, Bertakwa kepada Tuhan Yang Maha Esa, dan Berakhlak Mulia Faith, piety, and noble character

items: Religious education and practice
Moral reasoning and ethical decision-making
Respect for diversity of beliefs

steps: Berkebinekaan Global Global diversity appreciation

items: Intercultural competence and respect
Understanding Indonesian diversity (SARA \- ethnicity, religion, race, social groups)
Global citizenship and perspective-taking

steps: Bergotong Royong Collaboration and mutual assistance

items: Teamwork and collective problem-solving
Community service and social responsibility
Shared leadership

steps: Mandiri Independence and self-direction

items: Self-awareness and self-regulation
Initiative and agency
Resilience and adaptability

steps: Bernalar Kritis Critical reasoning

items: Analytical and reflective thinking
Evidence-based decision-making
Problem-solving and inquiry

steps: Kreatif Creativity and innovation

items: Original ideas and artistic expression
Design thinking
Experimentation and risk-taking

content.text: Implementation Across Eduversal Schools: Integration into SLOs: Eduversal Schoolwide Learner Outcomes (Section 1.2) intentionally align with Profil Pelajar Pancasila:

steps: Academic Excellence → Bernalar Kritis (Critical Reasoning)
Communication → Berkebinekaan Global (Global Diversity)
Global Competence → Berkebinekaan Global
Ethical Leadership → Beriman, Bertakwa, Berakhlak Mulia
Innovation → Kreatif (Creativity)
Wellbeing → Bergotong Royong, Mandiri

content.text: Both frameworks emphasize critical thinking, global competence, collaboration, and character. Profil Pelajar Pancasila \- Delivered Through EduSTEAM: The Profil Pelajar Pancasila program is the primary vehicle for developing Profil Pelajar Pancasila competencies. At Eduversal schools, projects are designed and delivered using the EduSTEAM framework, which integrates:

steps: STEAM disciplines: Students apply science, technology, engineering, arts, and mathematics to solve real-world challenges
Design Thinking: Empathize → Define → Ideate → Prototype → Test methodology guides project cycles
Maker Education: Hands-on creation, prototyping, and iteration using school makerspaces and digital fabrication tools
Inquiry-Based Learning: Students ask questions, investigate, and construct knowledge through exploration
Minimum 2 projects per academic year across all levels (per Kurikulum Merdeka guidelines)
SD (Primary): Age-appropriate themes with scaffolded STEAM activities (e.g., environmental care through simple engineering solutions, local wisdom through cultural arts integration)
SMP (Lower Secondary): More complex investigations requiring deeper research, data analysis, and technological solutions
SMA (Upper Secondary): Advanced projects integrating with CAS (Creativity, Activity, Service) and university-level research methodologies where applicable
Project themes selected from national Profil Pelajar Pancasila menu, including:
Sustainable living (Gaya Hidup Berkelanjutan)
Local wisdom (Kearifan Lokal)
Entrepreneurship (Kewirausahaan)
Diversity and tolerance (Bhinneka Tunggal Ika)
Student wellbeing (Kesejahteraan Diri)
Sustainable Living: Students design solar-powered solutions for local communities (Science \+ Technology \+ Engineering)
Local Wisdom: Document and preserve traditional crafts using digital media and create museum exhibits (Arts \+ Technology)
Entrepreneurship: Develop social enterprises using market research, financial modeling, and product design (Math \+ Arts \+ Technology)
Project Process (aligned with EduSTEAM pedagogy):
Empathize & Define: Students identify real community needs through interviews and observation
Research & Ideate: Investigate scientific/cultural background; brainstorm multiple solutions
Design & Prototype: Create tangible products, models, or solutions using maker tools
Test & Iterate: Gather feedback, refine designs, improve solutions
Showcase & Reflect: Present to authentic audiences (parents, community, local government); reflect on learning and Profil Pelajar Pancasila growth
Project proposals (tema, tujuan, tahapan, STEAM integration plan)
Student work portfolios (design journals, prototypes, photos/videos, presentations)
Reflection rubrics tracking growth in all 6 Profil Pelajar Pancasila dimensions
Exhibition materials (posters, digital presentations, physical displays)

content.text: Assessment based on Profil Pelajar Pancasila competencies, not subject grades:

steps: Rubrics assess development across the 6 dimensions
Portfolio-based assessment documenting process and growth
Self and peer reflection on collaboration, creativity, and character development
Annual EduSTEAM Summit for teachers (sharing best practices, project showcases)
Project mentors from Eduversal's STEAM specialist team available for consultation
Makerspace kits and digital tools provided by Eduversal central
Profil Pelajar Pancasila project database on EduNav platform with sample projects, rubrics, and lesson plans

content.text: Character Education Programs (Integrated with EduSTEAM): Beyond projects, Profil Pelajar Pancasila is woven throughout school culture and daily practices:

steps: Morning assemblies with Pancasila value reflection and student-led presentations
Service learning tied to Profil Pelajar Pancasila values and community needs
Student leadership development through peer mentoring, student council, and house systems
Restorative practices and peer mediation for conflict resolution
EduSTEAM clubs and competitions (robotics, coding, science olympiad, maker challenges) that reinforce innovation, collaboration, and critical thinking
Integrated STEAM activities across subject areas connecting Cambridge content with real-world Indonesian contexts
Project proposals with STEAM integration plans (tema, tujuan, tahapan, disiplin STEAM yang terintegrasi)
Student portfolios (design journals, process documentation, prototypes, reflections)
Photo/video documentation of making process and exhibitions
Assessment rubrics tracking Profil Pelajar Pancasila dimension growth
Community feedback and impact evidence
Annual EduSTEAM Summit participation records
Teacher training certificates (design thinking, maker education, STEAM pedagogy)
Makerspace usage logs and inventory
Student participation in STEAM competitions (EduOS, robotics, innovation challenges)
Sample interdisciplinary units integrating STEAM across subjects
Competency rubrics and progress tracking dashboards (per semester)
Integration maps showing Profil Pelajar Pancasila across curriculum, co-curricular, and culture
Student portfolios demonstrating growth in all 6 dimensions
Annual showcase/exhibition materials and community engagement records

content.text: Requirement: School calendars must align with the national academic calendar issued annually by the Ministry of Education, while accommodating Cambridge examination schedules. National Calendar Elements: Academic Year Structure:

items: Semester 1 (Ganjil): Mid-July to December
Semester 2 (Genap): January to mid-June
Minimum 38 instructional weeks per year (excluding holidays and exams)
content.title: Academic Calendar Alignment

items: Indonesian Independence Day (August 17)
Religious holidays (Idul Fitri, Idul Adha, Nyepi, Christmas, Waisak, Chinese New Year, etc.)
National Hero's Day (November 10)
Education Day (May 2)
Other national holidays as declared
Mid-semester break: \~1 week (typically October and March)
Semester break: \~2 weeks (end December/early January and June/July)
May/June (IGCSE, AS, A Level) (recommended)
October/November (IGCSE, AS, A Level)
Checkpoint exams as scheduled
Draft annual calendar by April for next academic year
Submit to Eduversal for review
Balance national requirements with Cambridge exam prep and administration
Communicate calendar to families in multiple languages (English \+ Bahasa Indonesia)
Annual academic calendar approved by GM and submitted to Eduversal
Comparison chart showing alignment with national and Cambridge calendars
Communication to families (newsletters, parent portal)

content.text: Requirement: Schools must meet minimum instructional hours per week as specified by national regulations, varying by level:

items: SD (Primary): Minimum 30-36 hours/week (depending on grade)
SMP (Lower Secondary): Minimum 38-40 hours/week
SMA (Upper Secondary): Minimum 38-40 hours/week
content.title: Instructional Hours (Jam Pelajaran)

items: 1 jam pelajaran \= 35-45 minutes (depending on school level and regulation)
Timetables designed to meet or exceed minimum hours
Both national and Cambridge subjects count toward total
Documented in master timetable reviewed by GM and submitted with operational license renewals
Master timetable by grade and subject
Calculation showing compliance with minimum hours
Attendance records and instructional delivery logs

subsections.title: Child Protection and Student Welfare Regulations

items: Law No. 35 of 2014 amending Law No. 23 of 2002 on Child Protection
Minister of Education Regulation on Prevention and Handling of Violence in Educational Settings
content.title: National Child Protection Compliance

content.text: Key Requirements: 1. Anti-Bullying and Violence Prevention:

items: Written anti-bullying policy accessible to all students and families
Clear reporting mechanisms for students, parents, and staff
Investigation and response protocols
Regular awareness campaigns and education for students
Training for all staff on recognizing and responding to bullying
Absolutely no physical punishment or violence against students
Positive behavior management and restorative practices mandated
Disciplinary procedures must respect student dignity
Physical safety measures (secure campus, controlled access, emergency procedures)
Psychological safety (inclusive culture, mental health support)
Online safety (AUP for technology use, cyberbullying prevention)
Suspected child abuse or neglect must be reported to authorities (police, social services)
School maintains confidential incident log
Cooperation with external investigations
Section 9 of this handbook details comprehensive safeguarding procedures
Indonesian regulations set the minimum standard; Eduversal policy often exceeds these requirements
Designated Safeguarding Lead (DSL) ensures compliance with both national law and Eduversal standards
Anti-bullying policy published in English and Bahasa Indonesia
Training records for staff on child protection and positive discipline
Incident logs and response documentation
Reporting protocols and contact information for authorities
Student and parent awareness materials

content.text: Requirement: Schools must monitor and report student attendance, with interventions for chronic absenteeism. Standards:

items: Minimum 90% attendance rate expected
Absences exceeding 10% (without valid reason) may result in retention or academic intervention
Parents must provide written excuse for absences
content.title: Attendance and Truancy

steps: Daily attendance taken and recorded in school information system (EduNav)
Absences exceeding 3 consecutive days trigger parent contact
Chronic absenteeism (\>10% over semester) → meeting with parents, counselor, and GM to develop support plan
Unresolved truancy may be reported to local authorities as per regulations

items: Attendance data in EduNav
Parent communication logs
Intervention plans and outcomes
Annual attendance summary by grade and cohort
PAUD: Diploma IV (D-IV) or Bachelor's (S1) in Early Childhood Education or related field
SD: S1 in Primary Education or relevant subject \+ teaching credential
SMP/SMA: S1 in subject area \+ teaching credential (Akta IV or equivalent)
content.title: Teacher Qualifications

items: Preference for teachers with Cambridge training or international school experience
EAL (English as an Additional Language) competency for teachers delivering Cambridge curriculum
All teachers must meet minimum national qualifications; Eduversal often recruits above these minimums
Must hold bachelor's degree and relevant teaching qualifications from home country
Subject to KITAS/KITAP work permit requirements (Section 2.8)
Teacher qualification matrix with copies of degrees, transcripts, and teaching credentials
Verification logs confirming the authenticity of documents
Cambridge training certificates (if applicable)

content.text: Purpose: NUPTK is a unique identifier for Indonesian teachers and education personnel, linked to certification, salary, and benefits. Eligibility:

items: Indonesian citizen
Holds required qualifications
Employed by school with operational license
content.title: NUPTK (Nomor Unik Pendidik dan Tenaga Kependidikan)

items: Submit through Dapodik system
Managed by local Dinas Pendidikan
Required for teacher certification (sertifikasi guru)
Access to government professional development programs
Potential eligibility for government allowances (if applicable to school type)

content.text: Note: Not all Eduversal teachers will have NUPTK (e.g., foreign teachers, some contract staff). The school maintains records for those who do. Evidence:

items: NUPTK records for eligible teachers
Application tracking for teachers in process

content.text: Requirement: All foreign nationals employed by Eduversal Partner Schools must hold valid work authorization from Indonesian immigration authorities. Key Permits: 1. KITAS (Kartu Izin Tinggal Terbatas) \- Limited Stay Permit:

items: Issued for specific employment with a sponsoring entity (the local Yayasan)
Valid for 1-2 years, renewable
Requires:
Sponsorship letter from Yayasan
Employment contract
Proof of qualifications
Health clearance
Security clearance
content.title: KITAS/KITAP and Work Permits for Expatriate Staff

content.text: 2. KITAP (Kartu Izin Tinggal Tetap) \- Permanent Stay Permit:

items: For long-term residents (typically after 5+ years on KITAS)
Allows continued work authorization without annual renewal

content.text: 3. Work Permit (IMTA \- Izin Mempekerjakan Tenaga Kerja Asing):

items: Employer (Yayasan) must obtain approval from Ministry of Manpower to employ a foreign worker
Justification required (specialized skills not available locally)
Position must comply with "Negative List" (positions restricted to Indonesian citizens)
Local Yayasan (legal sponsor and employer of record)
Eduversal HR team coordinates documentation and application
External immigration consultant may be engaged for complex cases
Maintain copies of all foreign staff passports, KITAS/KITAP, and work permits
Track expiration dates and initiate renewals 3 months in advance
Report any changes (address, marital status, employment terms) to immigration
Ensure foreign staff comply with visa conditions (no unauthorized work, proper exit/re-entry permits)
Passport valid for at least 18 months
Comply with reporting requirements (SKLD \- Surat Keterangan Lapor Diri) within 24 hours of arrival/address change
Obtain EPO (Exit Permit Only) or multiple re-entry permit for international travel
Maintain valid health and accident insurance
Spouses and children may be sponsored for dependent KITAS
School may assist with dependent applications per MOU agreements
Foreign staff immigration file for each expatriate
KITAS/KITAP and work permit copies
Renewal tracking spreadsheet
Correspondence with immigration authorities and consultants
SKLD reporting confirmations

content.text: Requirement: To mitigate legal and financial liabilities for the Yayasan, a formal termination protocol for all expatriate staff must be followed. Upon the conclusion of employment—whether through contract completion, resignation, or termination—the school must ensure the legal cancellation of the individual’s stay permit (KITAS/KITAP) and work authorization. The EPO Process:

items: Mandatory Filing: The school’s HR department or the local Yayasan must initiate the Exit Permit Only (EPO) process with the Indonesian Immigration Office.
7-Day Compliance Window: The EPO application must be submitted and processed to ensure the Yayasan's sponsorship is legally terminated within 7 days of the foreign employee’s final working day.

Sponsorship Release: Processing the EPO officially removes the employee from the Yayasan’s "Foreign Worker Manpower Plan" (RPTKA) and absolves the foundation of further legal responsibility for the individual's stay in Indonesia.
Departure Requirement: Once the EPO is stamped in the passport, the foreign national typically has 7 days to depart the country or transition to a new sponsor.
content.title: KITAS/KITAP Termination and EPO (Exit Permit Only) Protocol

items: School HR/GM: Must track the last day of service and provide necessary documents (original passport and KITAS card) to the immigration consultant or Yayasan legal team.
Final Settlement: Final salary payments or "End of Service" benefits should only be fully disbursed upon confirmation that the EPO process has been initiated or completed.
EPO Stamp/Certificate: A copy of the processed EPO stamp from the employee's passport or the electronic EPO notification.
Notification to Ministry of Manpower: Evidence that the work permit (IMTA/Notification) has been revoked in the TKA Online system.
Updated Tracking Log: The school’s foreign staff registry must reflect the "Cancelled" or "Terminated" status of the visa.

content.text: Requirement: Schools and staff must comply with Indonesian tax regulations, including withholding and reporting obligations. Key Tax Obligations: 1. PPh 21 (Income Tax on Employee Salaries):

items: Employer (Yayasan) withholds income tax from employee salaries
Monthly tax calculation based on progressive rates
Filed monthly via e-filing system
Annual tax reconciliation and issuance of Form 1721 A1/A2 to employees by January 31
content.title: Tax Compliance (PPh 21, PPh 23, VAT)

items: School withholds 2% tax on payments to service providers (consultants, contractors, vendors)
Remit to tax office and issue withholding receipt (bukti potong)
Educational services are generally VAT-exempt under Indonesian law
However, VAT may apply to non-educational ancillary services (e.g., canteen, bookstore, transportation)
School must determine VAT status in consultation with tax advisor
Foreign employees subject to same PPh 21 withholding as Indonesian staff
Tax treaty benefits may apply (school must verify and document)
Annual tax return (SPT Tahunan) required for foreign residents
Payroll calculation with PPh 21 withholding
E-filing of PPh 21 and PPh 23 returns
Payment of tax to bank or online
Issue Form 1721 A1/A2 to employees
File annual tax reconciliation
Corporate tax return for Yayasan (if applicable)
School finance team or outsourced payroll provider
GM oversight and approval
External tax consultant for advisory and audit support
Monthly tax returns (PPh 21, PPh 23) filed confirmations
Payment receipts
Employee tax forms (1721 A1/A2)
Correspondence with tax authorities
Tax compliance audit report (annual)

content.text: Requirement: All employees (Indonesian and foreign residents) must be enrolled in Indonesia's social security programs: 1. BPJS Kesehatan (Health Insurance):

items: Universal health coverage
Premium shared between employer and employee (varies by salary tier)
Covers employee and up to 5 dependents
content.title: BPJS (Social Security) Registration

items: Four programs:
JKK (Jaminan Kecelakaan Kerja) \- Work accident insurance
JKM (Jaminan Kematian) \- Death benefit
JHT (Jaminan Hari Tua) \- Old age savings
JP (Jaminan Pensiun) \- Pension (for employees earning above threshold)
Premiums vary by program and salary; split between employer and employee
Enroll new employees in BPJS Kesehatan and Ketenagakerjaan within first month of employment
Provide BPJS cards and information
Calculate and pay premiums online
Upload payroll data to BPJS systems
Update BPJS status when employee leaves
Final premium settlement
BPJS Kesehatan: Mandatory for all foreign residents with KITAS
BPJS Ketenagakerjaan: Required for expatriates in some programs; exemptions may apply based on reciprocity agreements
HR team or payroll provider
Finance team for premium payments
BPJS enrollment confirmations for all staff
Monthly premium payment receipts
BPJS compliance reports
Employee BPJS cards

content.text: Requirement: Every Eduversal Partner School registered as a Cambridge International Centre must appoint a designated Exams Officer (EO). This individual serves as the primary administrative link between the school and Cambridge International, ensuring that the integrity of all external assessments is maintained and that the school’s Centre status is never jeopardized. Key Responsibilities and the Cambridge Handbook:

items: Regulatory Compliance: The EO is responsible for reading, understanding, and implementing all regulations specified in the current year’s Cambridge Handbook.
Secure Storage ("Blue Box" Protocols): The EO must oversee the high-security storage of all confidential exam materials. This includes:
Ensuring a secure room with a permanent, non-combustible walls and a solid door with a high-security lock.
Maintaining a "two-key" system, where keys/codes are held only by authorized personnel.
Verifying that exam papers remain in their original, unopened blue outer-packaging until the time of the exam.
Exam Series Management: Coordination of the May/June and October/November exam series, including candidate entries, timetable management, and the secure dispatch of completed scripts.
Invigilator Training: Recruiting and training invigilators to ensure they are competent in identifying and preventing malpractice during examinations.
Centre Inspections: Preparing for and hosting unannounced Cambridge Centre inspections to demonstrate full compliance with international standards.
content.title: Cambridge Exams Officer (EO)

content.text: Operational Integration: The Exams Officer works closely with the Senior Leadership Team (SLT) to ensure that the academic calendar accounts for Cambridge exam periods and that the facilities (exam halls, computer labs) meet technical requirements. Evidence of Compliance:

items: Official Designation: Appointment letter for the Exams Officer role.
Training Records: Certificates of completion for Cambridge International administrative training.
Security Logs: Access logs for the secure storage facility and "two-key" holder registry.
Inspection Reports: Results and feedback from official Cambridge Centre inspections

content.text: Purpose: Local and provincial education offices conduct periodic inspections to verify compliance with operational standards, curriculum delivery, safety, and student welfare. Frequency:

items: Routine: Every 3-5 years as part of operational license renewal
Ad hoc: In response to complaints, incidents, or policy changes
Accreditation visit: Coordinated with BAN-S/M accreditation cycle (see below)
content.title: Dinas Pendidikan Inspections

items: Facilities and safety (buildings, labs, playgrounds, emergency exits)
Teacher qualifications and employment documentation
Curriculum and instructional quality (lesson plans, student work samples)
Student records and assessment
Financial management and transparency
Child protection policies and implementation
National subject integration

content.text: Note: Eduversal also conducts internal quality assurance visits separate from national accreditation. See Section 2.21 (Eduversal Quality Assurance Visits) for details on the annual appraisal process. Preparation: Principal Responsibilities:

steps: Maintain inspection-ready documentation at all times (policy register, evidence files)
Upon notice of inspection (typically 2-4 weeks advance):

items: Review checklist of required documents
Conduct internal pre-inspection audit
Brief staff on inspection protocols and expectations
Prepare tour route and demonstration lessons

steps: During inspection:

items: Host inspection team professionally
Provide requested documentation promptly
Answer questions transparently
Take notes on findings and recommendations

steps: Post-inspection:

items: Review inspection report
Develop action plan for any deficiencies or recommendations
Submit action plan to Dinas and Chief Academic Officer
Implement improvements and document progress
Follow up on deadlines (e.g., for corrective actions)
Inspection notices and correspondence
Inspection reports with findings and recommendations
Action plans and implementation logs
Follow-up documentation and closure letters

content.text: Purpose: BAN-S/M (Badan Akreditasi Nasional Sekolah/Madrasah) is the national accreditation body that assesses school quality against Indonesian standards. Accreditation Levels:

items: A (Unggul) \- Excellent: Score 91-100 / Score 96–100 (for SPK schools)
B (Baik) \- Good: Score 81-90 / Score 86–95 (for SPK schools)
C (Cukup) \- Adequate: Score 71-80 / Score 71–85 (for SPK schools)
TT (Tidak Terakreditasi) \- Not accredited: Score \<71
content.title: National Accreditation (BAN-PDM)

items: Schools are accredited every 5 years
Self-assessment and application submitted 6-12 months before the accreditation visit
Accreditation visit (2-3 days) by BAN-S/M assessors
Report and rating issued within 3 months

steps: Curriculum and Learning Process
Graduate Competency Standards (aligned to national outcomes)
Assessment Standards
Educators and Education Personnel
Facilities and Infrastructure
School Management
Financing
Educational Culture and Environment

content.text: Special Note for SPK (Satuan Pendidikan Kerjasama) Schools:

items: SPK schools are assessed under the same eight national standards, but their evaluation emphasizes international curriculum implementation and bilingual learning quality.

For SPK schools that achieve Grade A (96–100), BAN-S/M visitation may not be required in the next cycle, provided that all required documents and self-assessments are regularly uploaded and maintained in the national accreditation system (Sispena).

content.text: The Required Documents to Maintain the A Status To keep your accreditation active and automatically extended, the school must:

steps: Regularly update data on:

items: Teacher qualifications and certifications
Student academic outcomes (national & international)
Infrastructure, safety, and learning resources
Financial accountability and governance
Curriculum implementation (national \+ international for SPK)

steps: Submit annual reports or “Pemantauan Kinerja Sekolah” through Sispena-SPK
Respond to BAN-S/M monitoring requests or virtual verification if needed

content.text: If all data remain valid and performance indicators are maintained, the school’s accreditation automatically extends for another 5-year term without a full visitation. Strategy for Eduversal Partner Schools: Dual Accreditation Approach:

items: Pursue both National Accreditation (BAN-S/M) to satisfy Indonesian requirements
AND International Accreditation (CIS/WASC) to demonstrate global standards (Section 18)
Many BAN-S/M standards overlap with CIS/WASC criteria
Evidence collected for international accreditation often satisfies national requirements
National subject integration and Profil Pelajar Pancasila documentation crucial for BAN-S/M
All Eduversal schools aim for A (Unggul) accreditation from BAN-S/M
Maintain continuous improvement between cycles

steps: Principal leads school self-assessment using BAN-S/M instrument
Evidence compilation mapped to 8 national standards
Internal mock visit 3 months before official BAN-S/M visit
Staff training on BAN-S/M accreditation standards and interview protocols
Coordinate with Dinas Pendidikan for visit scheduling

items: BAN-S/M accreditation certificate (current)
Self-assessment report (ISED \- Instrumen Sekolah/Madrasah)
Accreditation visit schedule and assessor team info
Evidence portfolio mapped to 8 standards
Final accreditation report and improvement recommendations

content.text: Requirement: Schools must establish fair, transparent, and non-discriminatory tuition and fee structures. Best Practices (Indonesian Context):

items: Publish fee schedule annually (tuition, registration, materials, uniforms, trips, etc.)
Provide fee schedule in both English and Bahasa Indonesia
Offer scholarship or financial aid for qualifying families (per Eduversal equity commitment)
No hidden fees; all charges disclosed upfront
Receipts issued for all payments
Refund policy clearly stated (e.g., for withdrawal)
content.title: Tuition and Fee Regulations

items: Arbitrary or discriminatory fee increases
Withholding academic records or diplomas for non-payment (student welfare protected)
Coercive collection practices
Published fee schedule (website, prospectus, enrollment materials)
Fee approval documentation (reviewed by GM and Yayasan)
Scholarship policy and recipient records
Payment receipts and financial records
Communications regarding fee changes (with reasonable notice)

content.text: Requirement: Schools must conduct procurement transparently and ethically, avoiding conflicts of interest, corruption, or misuse of funds. Principles:

items: Competitive bidding for major contracts (e.g., \>IDR 50M threshold)
Written procurement policy with approval thresholds
Conflict of interest disclosure for decision-makers
Documentation of vendor selection rationale
Contracts in writing with clear scope, deliverables, pricing
content.title: Procurement and Anti-Corruption

items: EduNav platform used for expense requests and approvals
Multi-level approval for expenditures (unit head → department head → GM → finance)
Annual procurement plan aligned to approved budget
External audit of financial statements (annual)
Procurement policy
Vendor contracts and RFP documentation
Approval logs in EduNav
Audit reports confirming clean findings

content.text: Indonesia's geographic and climatic conditions require schools to prepare for natural disasters (earthquakes, floods, volcanic activity, tsunamis) and other emergencies (fire, pandemic, civil unrest). Mandatory Elements: 1. Emergency Response Plan:

items: Evacuation routes and assembly points clearly marked
Emergency contact tree (staff, families, authorities)
Roles and responsibilities (incident commander, first aid, communications)
Specific protocols for earthquake, fire, flood, etc.
content.title: Requirement

items: Fire drill: Minimum 2x per year
Earthquake drill: Minimum 2x per year
Other drills as relevant to location (tsunami, flood)
Document drill participation, timing, and lessons learned
First aid kits in key locations (office, clinic, classrooms)
Emergency food and water (3-day supply for students/staff who may need to shelter in place)
Flashlights, battery-powered radio, megaphone
Fire extinguishers (inspected annually)
Emergency notification system (SMS, app, email) to alert families
Liaison with local disaster management agency (BPBD)
Backup plan for remote learning if campus closed (leveraging EduNav and digital tools)
Academic calendar adjustments in case of extended closure
Emergency response plan (Indonesian and English versions)
Drill schedule and debrief reports
Emergency supply inventory and maintenance logs
Communication templates and contact lists
Training records for first aid and emergency response

content.text: Context: Although pandemic intensity has decreased, schools must remain prepared to implement health protocols as directed by the Ministry of Health and the Ministry of Education. Compliance Areas: 1. Health Screening:

items: Temperature checks at entry (if required)
Symptom monitoring and isolation protocols
Contact tracing in case of positive cases
Handwashing stations and sanitizers
Cleaning and disinfection protocols (high-touch surfaces)
Waste management (especially medical waste)
Adjustments to class size or hybrid learning
Staggered schedules
Physical distancing markers
Encourage vaccination per government recommendations (not mandatory but promoted)
Maintain vaccination records if relevant for outbreak management
Clear health and safety guidelines shared with families (English \+ Bahasa Indonesia)
Transparency about cases while protecting individual privacy
Regular updates aligned with government announcements
Blended learning infrastructure maintained (EduNav, video conferencing, digital resources)
Teacher training on online pedagogy
Device and connectivity support for students in need
Health protocols adjusted based on government directives (national, provincial, or local)
GM monitors official announcements and implements changes promptly
Coordination with the Chief Academic Officer for network-wide consistency
Health protocol policy (current version aligned with government requirements)
Screening records (if applicable)
Cleaning schedules and supply inventory
Communication to families regarding health measures
Remote learning contingency plan
Staff training on health protocols
Law No. 27 of 2022 on Personal Data Protection (PDP Law) \- Indonesia's comprehensive data privacy law
Minister of Communication and Information Technology regulations on electronic data
Education sector-specific guidance on student records
content.title: Legal Framework:

content.text: Key Principles: 1. Lawful Basis for Processing:

items: Consent from data subject (parents for minors)
Legal obligation (e.g., reporting to Dapodik)
Legitimate interest (school operations)
Personal data collected only for specified, explicit purposes
No secondary use without additional consent
Collect only data necessary for educational purposes
Keep data accurate and up to date
Retain only as long as necessary; establish retention schedule
Technical and organizational measures to protect data
Access controls, encryption for sensitive data
Incident response plan for data breaches
Access to personal data
Correction of inaccurate data
Deletion (right to be forgotten) under certain conditions
Portability (transfer records to another school)
Maintain register of personal data processed (students, parents, staff, visitors)
Categories: name, address, contact info, academic records, health data, financial data, etc.
Inform families and staff how data will be used
Published in enrollment materials, employee handbook, website
Available in English and Bahasa Indonesia
Obtain explicit consent for non-essential data processing (e.g., photos for marketing, participation in research)
Separate consent for each purpose
Parents may withdraw consent
Limit sharing to authorized recipients (Dapodik, Eduversal HQ, accreditation bodies, government authorities)
Data Processing Agreements (DPAs) with vendors (EduNav, payroll provider, etc.) ensuring they protect data
Annual training on data protection responsibilities
Confidentiality agreements signed by all staff
Data breach protocol: contain, assess, notify affected parties and authorities (if required), remediate
Breach log maintained
Conduct DPIA before implementing new systems or processes involving sensitive data (Section 14 details)
Personal data register
Privacy notices (English \+ Bahasa Indonesia)
Consent forms (enrollment, photo release, etc.)
Data Processing Agreements with vendors
Staff confidentiality agreements and training records
Breach log and incident response documentation
DPIAs for major systems (e.g., EduNav implementation)

subsections.title: Reporting Obligations to Yayasan and Eduversal

content.text: Purpose: Keep the local Yayasan (school owner) informed of operational performance, financial status, and compliance. Frequency:

items: Mid-Year Report: End of Semester 1 (December/January)
End-of-Year Report: End of Semester 2 (June/July)
content.title: Bi-Annual Reporting to Local Yayasan

items: Enrollment data (current, trends, projections)
Staff headcount and any significant changes
Facilities and infrastructure updates
Key events and milestones
Student achievement data (internal assessments, Cambridge results, Profil Pelajar Pancasila projects)
Progress against School Improvement Plan (SIP) goals
Curriculum developments and innovations
Budget vs. actual (revenue and expenditure)
Major variances and explanations
Cash flow and reserves
Licensing and accreditation renewals
Dapodik and SIMPKB updates
Ministry inspections or audits
Any incidents or issues (safeguarding, health/safety, legal)
Issues requiring Yayasan support or decision
Strategic considerations
Capital investment needs
Written report (10-15 pages) with appendices (data tables, charts)
Oral presentation to Yayasan Board or leadership (60-90 minutes)
Q\&A session
Bi-annual report documents (archived for 5 years)
Meeting minutes with Yayasan
Follow-up action items and tracking

content.text: Purpose: Enable network-wide oversight, quality assurance, benchmarking, and support. Frequency:

items: Quarterly: High-level dashboard (enrollment, finance, key metrics)
Bi-Annual: Comprehensive report (aligned with Yayasan reporting)
Ad Hoc: Critical incidents, urgent issues, or requests for support
content.title: Reporting to the Eduversal Chief Academic Officer

items: Status of goals, KPIs, and success criteria
Evidence of impact on student learning

steps: DTP activities and teacher participation
Appraisal results and trends
EduOS participation and outcomes
EduNav utilization metrics
Cycle-by-cycle participation rates
School-wide trends across all 4 cycles
Comparison to network averages
Interventions implemented based on data
Impact evidence (growth from Cycle 1 to Cycle 4)

items: Current roster (rotasi and non-rotasi)
Vacancies and recruitment needs
Appraisal outcomes (overall trends, not individual data)
Rotation recommendations for the next cycle
Exam registrations and results (Checkpoint, IGCSE, AS/A)
Any irregularities or special considerations
Centre status and inspections
Actual vs. budget
Tuition collection rate
Any financial concerns or requests
Operational, academic, or HR issues
Requests for Eduversal intervention or resources
Structured report template provided by Eduversal
Submitted via secure platform (EduNav or Google Drive)
Reviewed by the Chief Academic Officer and relevant specialists
Feedback and action plan provided to GM within 2 weeks
Quarterly dashboards and bi-annual reports (archived)
Email confirmations of submission
Feedback and action plans from Eduversal
Implementation logs for recommendations

content.text: Purpose: Maintain a centralized, accessible repository of all policies, regulations, licenses, and compliance evidence. Policy Register Contents: Category 1: Legal and Regulatory:

items: Yayasan Akta Pendirian and amendments
Operational licenses (PAUD, SD, SMP, SMA) \- current and historical
NPSN certificate
BAN-S/M accreditation certificate
Ministry inspection reports (past 5 years)
Immigration documents (foreign staff KITAS/KITAP tracking)
National subject syllabi and lesson plans
Profil Pelajar Pancasila project documentation
Academic calendar (national alignment)
Instructional hours compliance calculation
Teacher qualification verification records
NUPTK register
Employment contracts (template and samples)
BPJS enrollment records
Tax compliance reports (PPh 21, PPh 23)
Child protection policy (English \+ Bahasa Indonesia)
Anti-bullying policy
Emergency response plan
Drill logs
Health protocol policy (COVID-19 or successor)
Incident logs (safeguarding, accidents, emergencies)
Annual budget and Yayasan approval
QMS fee schedule and payment records
Procurement policy
Audit reports (financial, compliance)
Privacy notices
Consent forms
Data Processing Agreements
Breach log
Bi-annual reports to Yayasan
Quarterly and bi-annual reports to Eduversal
Dapodik and SIMPKB submission receipts
EAL Entry and Exit Benchmarks
SEN Identification and Support (IEP/LSP)
Policy Register Owner: General Manager (overall) with designated staff support (e.g., Administrative Manager or Compliance Officer)
Storage: Secure digital repository (Google Drive, EduNav, or similar) with controlled access
Backup: Offsite backup and hard copy archive for critical documents
Review Cycle: Annual review of all policies; updates as regulations change
Version Control: Track policy revisions with dates and change logs
GM and SLT: Full access
Department heads: Access to relevant sections
Teaching staff: Access to instructional and safeguarding policies
Families: Public-facing policies available on website (privacy notice, child protection summary, complaints procedure)
Auditors/Inspectors: Provided on request
Policy register index (spreadsheet or database)
Document library with version control
Access logs (who viewed/edited what)
Annual review checklist and sign-off
Inclusion Policy: Comprehensive document outlining EAL/SEN philosophies, aligned with CIS/WASC

content.text: Requirement: Schools must provide accessible, fair, and transparent procedures for students, parents, and staff to raise concerns or complaints. Indonesian Regulatory Context:

items: Parents have the right to be heard regarding their children's education
Students have rights to fair treatment and due process
Staff have labor rights and whistleblower protections

content.text: Eduversal Complaints Policy (aligned with Indonesian requirements): Scope: Covers complaints related to:

items: Academic issues (grading, curriculum, instruction)
Behavioral/disciplinary concerns
Facilities and services
Staff conduct or performance
Financial matters (fees, billing)
Discrimination, harassment, or bullying
Safeguarding concerns (separate expedited process \- Section 9)
Complainant raises concern with most relevant staff member (teacher, counselor, department head)
Staff member attempts to resolve informally within 5 working days
If unresolved or complainant unsatisfied → escalate to Level 2
Complainant submits written complaint (form available in English and Bahasa Indonesia)
Principal investigates (interviews, reviews evidence) within 10 working days
Written response provided with findings and proposed resolution
If unresolved or complainant unsatisfied → escalate to Level 3
Complainant appeals in writing to GM within 5 working days of Principal's response
GM reviews case (may appoint independent investigator if conflict of interest)
Decision rendered within 10 working days
GM decision is final at school level
If unresolved or complainant unsatisfied → escalate to Level 4
Serious or unresolved complaints may be escalated to:
Local Yayasan Board (for matters related to Yayasan obligations)
Chief Academic Officer (for matters related to academic standards, QMS, or network policies)
Review and decision within 15 working days
External mediation may be offered
Final decision communicated in writing
If complaint involves legal violations (labor law, child protection, discrimination), parties may seek remedy through:
Local Dinas Pendidikan
Ministry of Education (if Dinas resolution unsatisfactory)
Labor courts (for employment disputes)
Police (for criminal matters)
Any complaint involving child abuse, serious harm, or safeguarding risk is immediately escalated to Designated Safeguarding Lead (DSL) and may bypass normal process
External reporting to authorities as required by law (Section 9)
Complaints handled confidentially to extent possible
No retaliation against complainant (staff, student, or parent) for raising concern in good faith
Malicious or vexatious complaints may result in consequences
Complaints log maintained by Principal/GM (anonymous summary for serious complaints)
Individual complaint files with documentation of investigation and resolution
Annual report to Yayasan and Eduversal on complaint trends and systemic issues
Complaints policy (English \+ Bahasa Indonesia) published on website and in parent/student handbook
Complaint form template
Complaints log (anonymized summary)
Sample complaint files (with personal data redacted if needed for review)
Training records for staff on handling complaints
Annual complaints summary report

content.text: Purpose: ANBK replaced the Ujian Nasional (UN) as Indonesia's national assessment. It measures student competencies, learning environment quality, and school performance. Components: 1. Asesmen Kompetensi Minimum (AKM) \- Minimum Competency Assessment:

items: Tests literacy (reading comprehension) and numeracy (mathematical reasoning)
Not tied to specific curriculum content; focuses on applied skills
Administered to sample of students at grades 5, 8, and 11
content.title: ANBK (Asesmen Nasional Berbasis Komputer)

items: Measures student wellbeing and Profil Pelajar Pancasila values
Self-report survey on behaviors, attitudes, and school climate

content.text: 3. Survei Lingkungan Belajar \- Learning Environment Survey:

items: Completed by teachers and principals
Assesses instructional quality, leadership, and school culture
Computer-based (schools must provide devices and internet)
Conducted annually (typically August-September)
Students randomly selected (not all students take ANBK)
Not used for student grading or graduation
School-level results used for improvement planning and national benchmarking
Results available via national portal (Rapor Pendidikan)
Ensure computer lab and internet capacity adequate for ANBK
Coordinate scheduling with Dinas Pendidikan
Train proctors and technical staff
Communicate purpose and process to students and families (minimize anxiety; emphasize growth focus)
Follow national protocols for secure and fair test administration
Technical troubleshooting support
Proctor oversight to maintain integrity
Review school-level results (comparison to national, provincial, similar schools)
Identify strengths and areas for improvement (literacy, numeracy, character, learning environment)
Integrate findings into School Improvement Plan (SIP)
Share results with stakeholders transparently (parents, staff)
ANBK coordination correspondence with Dinas
Technical readiness checklist and test
Proctor training records
ANBK results report (from national portal)
SIP addendum or revision reflecting ANBK findings

content.text: Purpose: EASE is Eduversal's flagship standardized assessment program designed to measure student progress, evaluate teaching effectiveness, and provide data-driven insights for continuous improvement across all partner schools. It is one of the most prestigious services Eduversal provides to schools as part of the Quality Management System (QMS). Program Overview: EASE is a comprehensive, computer-based assessment platform that evaluates student achievement in both Cambridge curriculum and Indonesian national subjects. It provides detailed analytics to support personalized learning, inform instructional decisions, and prepare schools for accreditation. Provider: PT. Edukasi Aditama Indonesia (Edutama), an Eduversal partner company, delivers EASE using advanced technology and assessment expertise. Key Features: Four Annual Assessment Cycles:

steps: EASE is conducted 4 times per academic year for core subjects
Spaced throughout the year to track progress and inform intervention
Results available immediately after each cycle via online platform
content.title: EASE (Evaluation of Achievement Standardized Exam)

steps: English (Reading, Grammar, Listening, Writing)
Mathematics
Science (Primary/Lower Secondary)
Biology, Chemistry, Physics (Upper Secondary)
Religion and Moral Education
Individual Student Reports: Detailed breakdown of strengths, weaknesses, topic proficiency, and cognitive skill levels
Class Reports: Aggregate data showing class performance trends and comparison
Teacher Performance Insights: Data on instructional effectiveness and areas for targeted professional development
School-Wide Analytics: Longitudinal data for benchmarking across Eduversal network and informing School Improvement Plans
Cambridge Assessment standards and progression pathways
Kurikulum Merdeka competencies and Profil Pelajar Pancasila dimensions
CIS/WASC accreditation evidence (data-driven decision-making, assessment literacy)
Identifies students requiring intervention or extension
Topic-level analysis guides targeted instruction
Tracks EAL learner progress in language acquisition

content.text: School Responsibilities: Preparation (Led by School EASE Committee):

steps: Designate School EASE Coordinator (typically Academic Coordinator or Vice Principal)
Ensure all students enrolled in EduNav with accurate grade/subject data
Confirm device readiness (computers/tablets meeting minimum system requirements)
Test internet connectivity and backup solutions (mobile hotspots if needed)
Train invigilators on EASE protocols and platform navigation
Communicate EASE schedule and expectations to students and parents
Provide secure, distraction-free exam environment
Assign trained invigilators to supervise sessions (1 invigilator per 20-25 students)
Ensure students bring required materials (headphones for listening tests, scratch paper, calculators where permitted)
Monitor for technical issues and activate troubleshooting protocols
Maintain exam integrity (prevent cheating, unauthorized materials, device misuse)
Download and review reports from EASE platform (latihan.id or EduNav)
Data Analysis Meeting within 1 week of results release:
SLT reviews school-wide trends and identifies priority areas
Departments analyze subject-specific data and plan interventions
Teachers review individual and class reports to adjust instruction
Update intervention programs for underperforming students
Modify lesson plans based on topic-level proficiency gaps
Celebrate high achievers and share effective teaching practices
Share individual reports with families via EduNav parent portal
Hold parent conferences to discuss results and home support strategies

content.text: Benefits by Stakeholder: For School Management:

steps: Evaluate teaching and learning effectiveness across departments
Benchmark performance against other Eduversal schools (anonymized data)
Identify school-wide strengths and improvement priorities for SIP
Provide evidence for BAN-S/M and CIS/WASC accreditation (data use, assessment quality)
Demonstrate educational value to parents and prospective families
Identify specific student learning gaps and misconceptions
Receive external validation of classroom observations and formative assessments
Inform differentiation strategies and grouping decisions
Track impact of instructional changes over multiple cycles
Guide targeted professional development needs
Receive personalized feedback on strengths and areas for growth
Build self-awareness of learning progress and goal-setting skills
Experience standardized test format (preparation for Cambridge exams, ANBK)
See progress over time, boosting motivation and self-efficacy
Gain objective, detailed insights into child's academic progress
Understand specific skills/topics requiring additional support at home
Compare child's growth across multiple assessment points
Engage in informed conversations with teachers during conferences
SIP Alignment: EASE data informs annual School Improvement Plan goals and success criteria (e.g., "Increase Cycle 4 Mathematics proficiency by 10% for Grade 8")
Teacher Appraisal: Aggregate EASE results (not individual teacher data) contribute to school-wide evaluation of instructional quality during Eduversal Appraisal visits
Curriculum Review: Multi-year EASE trends guide curriculum mapping adjustments and vertical alignment efforts
Professional Development: DTP workshops and PLCs use EASE data to focus on high-need instructional areas (e.g., writing instruction, problem-solving strategies)

content.text: EASE complements these assessments by providing frequent, standardized, and actionable data that bridges formative and summative purposes. Evidence:

steps: EASE administration schedule and completion logs (per cycle)
Student participation data (enrolled vs. tested)
Individual, class, and school reports downloaded from platform
Data analysis meeting minutes (SLT, departments, PLCs)
Intervention plans developed from EASE insights
Parent communication samples (report sharing, conference notes)
SIP addendums or revisions reflecting EASE findings
Professional development plans targeting EASE-identified needs
Year-over-year trend analysis (longitudinal data)
EASE Handbook (detailed procedures for schools)
Eduversal EASE Committee: Central support team coordinating cycles, troubleshooting technical issues, and providing data interpretation training
EduNav Integration: EASE results automatically sync to student profiles for holistic progress tracking
Training Webinars: Annual training for School EASE Coordinators and invigilators
CIS/WASC: Assessment for Learning; Data-Driven Decision-Making; Evidence of Student Learning
Cambridge Centre Requirements: Use of assessment data to inform teaching and learning
Indonesian Regulations: Complementary to ANBK; supports school-based assessment autonomy
Eduversal QMS: Core component of quality assurance; evaluated during Appraisal visits

content.text: Critical Note: EASE is not used for individual student grading or teacher performance evaluation in isolation. It is a diagnostic and improvement tool designed to enhance learning, not a high-stakes accountability mechanism. Schools must use EASE data responsibly, focusing on growth over time rather than punitive comparisons.
Context: Schools now have greater autonomy in determining graduation requirements following the elimination of mandatory national exams (UN). Requirement:

items: Schools must establish clear, rigorous graduation criteria aligned with national graduate competency standards
Criteria must include academic achievement, character development (Profil Pelajar Pancasila), and completion of required subjects
content.title: School Examination and Graduation Requirements (USBN Equivalent)

content.text: Eduversal Partner Schools Approach: Graduation Requirements (SMA example): 1. Academic Achievement:

items: Pass all required subjects (Cambridge \+ National) with minimum grades:
Cambridge IGCSE subjects: Minimum grade C (or 4 in new 9-1 scale) in core subjects
Cambridge AS/A Levels: Minimum grade D for AS, C for A Level (varies by university requirements)
Indonesian National subjects: Minimum score 70/100
Cumulative GPA across three years ≥ 2.75 (on 4.0 scale)
Complete minimum 5 IGCSE subjects (including English, Mathematics, Science)
Complete minimum 3 AS or A Level subjects (for students pursuing university admission)
Demonstrate proficiency in Bahasa Indonesia, PPKn, Religion, and Mulok
Assessed through school-based exams and projects
Complete minimum 2 (Projek Penguatan Profil Pelajar Pancasila) projects per year (total 6 over 3 years)
Demonstrate growth across Profil Pelajar Pancasila dimensions through portfolio assessment
Participate in minimum 1 co-curricular activity per year
Complete 60 hours of community service over 3 years
No major disciplinary infractions; resolve any outstanding issues
Positive behavior record
All fees paid (or payment plan agreed)
Library books and school property returned
Students meeting all requirements receive school diploma (Ijazah)
Cambridge certificates issued by Cambridge Assessment International Education separately
Transcript listing all subjects, grades, and achievements
Graduation requirements policy (published in student handbook, website)
Student transcripts and grade reports
Profil Pelajar Pancasila project portfolios
Co-curricular and service logs
Graduation checklist for each student (completed senior year)
Ijazah and transcript templates

content.text: Purpose: Eduversal conducts regular quality assurance visits (Supervisi) to all partner schools to monitor standards, provide coaching, and ensure continuous improvement across the network. Frequency:

items: Minimum 3 visits per school per academic year
Additional visits as needed for new schools, turnaround situations, or accreditation preparation
Classroom observations using the Eduversal teaching standards rubric
Review of lesson plans and assessment practices
Student work samples and evidence of differentiation
EAL/SEN support implementation
Cambridge curriculum coverage and pacing
Integration of national subjects (Pancasila, Bahasa Indonesia, Religion, Mulok)
Profil Pelajar Pancasila project quality and documentation
Vertical alignment and progression
Internal moderation practices
EASE results analysis and response
Cambridge Progression Tests and mock exam data
Use of assessment to inform instruction
DTP participation and impact
Appraisal cycle compliance and quality
EduOS enrollment and outcomes
EduNav utilization and data quality
Safeguarding policy implementation
DSL capacity and incident management
Student support systems (counseling, pastoral care)
Inclusive practices for diverse learners
Indonesian regulatory compliance (licenses, Dapodik, reporting)
Cambridge Centre standards adherence
Financial management and budget execution
Facilities and resources adequacy
Eduversal team notifies GM of visit dates and focus areas
School prepares evidence portfolio and self-assessment
Schedule shared (classroom observations, meetings, document review)
Entry meeting with GM and SLT
Classroom observations (random sample \+ targeted)
Document review (policies, student work, data dashboards)
Interviews with staff, students, and parents (as needed)
Facility walkthrough
Daily debriefs with leadership
Written report with commendations and recommendations
Action plan co-developed with GM
Follow-up timeline and accountability measures
Share findings with Yayasan (summary)

content.text: Appraisal Domains: Eduversal Appraisal evaluates schools across 5 key domains:

steps: School Development: Mission, vision, strategic planning, stakeholder engagement
School Management and Leadership: Governance, decision-making, resource allocation, staff supervision
Subject Departments Structure and Professional Development: Department organization, teacher collaboration, CPD effectiveness
Olympiad and Educational Technology: EduOS program, STEAM initiatives, technology integration
Curriculum, Teaching and Assessment: Lesson quality, differentiation, assessment practices, student engagement

content.text: For detailed expectations and evidence requirements for each domain, refer to the School Appraisal System Handbook. Evidence:

items: Supervisi visit schedule (annual)
Visit reports with commendations and recommendations
School action plans and implementation logs
Follow-up correspondence and monitoring
CIS/WASC: Leadership for Continuous Improvement; Quality Assurance
Internal accountability and network coherence

content.text: Context: Indonesian universities and government agencies increasingly recognize Cambridge qualifications, but schools must ensure students and families understand the equivalency for local university admission and beyond. Cambridge IGCSE Equivalency:

items: Recognized as equivalent to Indonesian SMA completion for university admission purposes
Students typically need 5+ IGCSE passes (grades A-C) for competitive university admission
Some Indonesian universities require additional Indonesian national subjects (Bahasa Indonesia, PPKn, Religion) for admission \- Eduversal schools fulfill this by teaching national curriculum
Recognized for direct entry to undergraduate programs at many Indonesian and international universities
AS Level ≈ 1 year post-secondary study
A Level ≈ 2 years post-secondary; may grant advanced standing

content.text: Key Actions for Schools: 1. University Counseling:

items: Provide guidance on university pathways (Indonesian, regional, international)
Explain Cambridge vs. national equivalency
Assist with applications and documentation (Section 23)
Issue comprehensive transcripts showing both Cambridge and national subjects
Provide letters of equivalency or school profile explaining curriculum (if requested by universities)
Stay updated on Ministry of Education and Ministry of Research & Higher Education policies regarding Cambridge recognition
Advocate through Eduversal network and associations (e.g., ISANA \- Indonesian Schools Association) for continued recognition
University admission outcomes data (where graduates matriculated)
Transcript and equivalency letter templates
University counseling records
Correspondence with Ministry or universities regarding recognition

content.text: To ensure nothing falls through the cracks, GMs should use this annual checklist:

items: ☐ Operational licenses current (check expiration dates; initiate renewals if within 6 months)
☐ NPSN data updated in Dapodik (start-of-year enrollment, staff changes)
☐ Academic calendar finalized and submitted to Dinas
☐ All staff BPJS Kesehatan and Ketenagakerjaan active
☐ Foreign staff KITAS/work permits valid (track expirations; start renewals 3 months early)
☐ Anti-bullying and safeguarding training for all staff (annual refresh)
☐ Emergency drills scheduled (fire, earthquake)
☐ EduSTEAM projects planned for the academic year (minimum 2 EduSTEAM-integrated projects with national themes selected)
☐ EduSTEAM resources and makerspace kits prepared for project implementation
☐ BAN-S/M accreditation timeline check (if visit scheduled within next 12 months, begin preparation)
☐ Eduversal Appraisal visit 1 scheduled and evidence prepared
content.title: Q1 (July-September):

items: ☐ ANBK administered (if applicable year/grade)
☐ Semester 1 internal exams and Cambridge Progression Tests
☐ Dapodik mid-year sync (by November 15)
☐ Mid-year financial review (budget vs. actual)
☐ Staff appraisals mid-cycle check-in
☐ Parent-teacher conferences
☐ Bi-annual report to Yayasan (end of semester 1)
☐ Bi-annual report to Eduversal
☐ Eduversal Appraisal visit completed; action plan from Visit 1 reviewed
☐ EASE Cycle 1 and Cycle 2 administered and data analysis completed
☐ Intervention plans updated based on EASE Cycle 1-2 results
content.title: Q2 (October-December):

items: ☐ SIMPKB updated (teacher CPD records)
☐ Tax reconciliation and 1721 forms issued to employees (by January 31)
☐ PPh 21 and PPh 23 annual returns filed
☐ QMS fee payment for the calendar year
☐ Policy register review (update any policies changed by regulation or practice)
☐ Draft next FY budget (for July start)
☐ Cambridge mock exams (for May/June IGCSE, AS/A)
☐ BAN-S/M self-assessment (ISED) updated if accreditation year
☐ EASE Cycle 3 administered; mid-year progress reviewed
content.title: Q3 (January-March):

items: ☐ Cambridge external exams (May/June series)
☐ Dapodik end-of-year sync (by May 15)
☐ Semester 2 internal exams
☐ Staff appraisals finalized
☐ Graduation (SMA/SMP) and promotion ceremonies
☐ Bi-annual report to Yayasan (end of academic year)
☐ Bi-annual report to Eduversal
☐ Enrollment for next academic year finalized
☐ Staff contracts renewed or rotasi decisions confirmed
☐ Facility maintenance and repairs (during break)
☐ Accreditation timeline check (if visit scheduled next year, prepare)
☐ Eduversal Appraisal visit completed; annual QMS summary report to Eduversal
☐ BAN-S/M accreditation visit (if scheduled); post-visit action plan submitted to Dinas
☐ EASE Cycle 4 administered; annual longitudinal analysis completed
☐ EASE data integrated into end-of-year SIP review and next-year planning
content.title: Q4 (April-June):

items: ☐ Ministry inspection preparation (if notified)
☐ Safeguarding incidents reported and managed per protocol
☐ Complaints logged and resolved per policy
☐ Data breach protocol activated if needed
☐ Health protocol updates (if government guidance changes)
content.title: Ongoing/As Needed:

content.text: Compliance Portfolio for Inspections/Audits: Legal Foundation:

items: Yayasan Akta and registration
NPWP certificate
Operational licenses (all levels)
NPSN certificate
Dapodik sync receipts (past 2 years)
SIMPKB records for certified teachers
Bi-annual reports to Yayasan and Eduversal
National subject lesson plans and syllabi
Profil Pelajar Pancasila project documentation
Academic calendar (national alignment)
Instructional hours calculation
EASE reports (all 4 cycles) \- individual, class, and school-wide
EASE data analysis meeting minutes and action plans
Intervention tracking logs informed by EASE
ANBK results and SIP integration
Cambridge Progression Test data (if applicable)
Teacher qualification matrix with copies
NUPTK records
KITAS/work permit files (foreign staff)
Employment contracts (samples)
BPJS enrollment confirmations
Tax records (PPh 21 returns, 1721 forms)
Safeguarding policy
Anti-bullying policy
Training records
Incident logs (anonymized summaries)
Emergency response plan
Drill logs
Health protocol policy
First aid and emergency supply inventory
Annual budgets and Yayasan approvals
QMS fee receipts
Audit reports

steps: BAN-S/M certificate (current) \- 5-year cycle
BAN-S/M self-assessment report (ISED)
CIS/WASC status (if applicable)
Eduversal Appraisal reports (annual \- minimum 3 visits per year)
Action plans from Eduversal Appraisal visits

items: Complaints policy
Complaints log (anonymized summary)
Privacy notices
Consent forms
Data Processing Agreements
CIS Standards: Operational Systems; Compliance; Governance (regulatory adherence)
WASC Category D: Resource Management & Development (legal compliance, facilities, financial practices)
WASC Category E: School Culture & Environment (safety, health, child protection)
Indonesian Regulations: Full compliance with national education laws, MoE regulations, labor law, tax law, immigration, health & safety, data protection
Cambridge Centre Requirements: National regulatory compliance as prerequisite for Centre status
